label: FP
reasoning: azericard.py:41 (bank_data) — `nonce = substr(hashlib.md5(str(irand).encode()).hexdigest(), 0, 16)`. MD5 here is being used as a hex formatter to produce 16 hex characters from an integer; no security property of MD5 (collision/preimage resistance) is relied upon. Authentication of the request is done by `hash_hmac('sha1', to_sign, key_for_sign)` on line 52. The MD5 call leaks no key material and creates no attack surface — it is purely a format transform. This is a False Positive on the MD5 weak-hash detector (Bandit B303), not a NON_ACTIONABLE protocol mandate. The AzeriCard MPI spec mandates a 16-hex NONCE field, not MD5 specifically; per the project CLAUDE.md guidance (sec. 3d 'stronger wrapper' / non-security use), MD5 used purely for byte-shaping is FP.

label: TP
reasoning: azericard.py:40 (bank_data) — `irand = random.randint(1, 10000000)`. This integer is the SOLE entropy source for the NONCE used in 3-D Secure MPI. ~23.25 bits of entropy for what the AzeriCard spec example shows as a 64-bit (16 hex) nonce. Although p_sign HMAC-SHA1 (line 52) authenticates the request and the merchant key is the security boundary, the NONCE in MPI 3DSecure is a freshness/anti-replay value, and a 23-bit RNG creates birthday collisions around ~3,200 transactions per terminal and is fully enumerable by any attacker with offline observation. The developer is NOT bound by the protocol to use `random.randint`; the spec mandates only the 16-hex wire format. A one-line swap to `secrets.token_hex(8)` (or `os.urandom(8).hex()`) satisfies the wire format with full 64-bit entropy. Therefore the alert is actionable. TP, not NON_ACTIONABLE. The original NA reasoning conflates 'spec mandates a NONCE field' with 'spec mandates MD5(randint)' — only the former is true.
